ui.subtitle: Quick reference for cards & rules. Click on the Catan cover for the official rules.
ui.footer: Community reference — not affiliated with Catan Studio.

reference.heading: Numeric Cheat Sheet
reference.subheading: Hard numbers you can glance at mid-turn.
reference.diceNote: Red numbers (6 and 8) have the highest per-roll chance. A 7 rolls the robber.

strategy.heading: Strategy Guide
strategy.subheading: Tips grouped by level. Use the pills to filter, or overlay tips from other expansions.
strategy.empty: No strategy tips for this selection yet.

tools.heading: Interactive Tools
tools.subheading: Small helpers — dice odds, starting-spot scoring, and robber tracking.

diceOdds.title: Dice Odds Calculator
diceOdds.intro: Tap the numbers on your hexes to see combined production odds.
diceOdds.hint: Left-click to add a hex, right-click to remove one. 7 is excluded (robber roll).
diceOdds.empty: Add some numbers to see results.

startingSpot.title: Starting-Spot Helper
startingSpot.intro: Pick the three hexes meeting at your candidate intersection. Score = pips × diversity − desert penalty.

robberTimer.title: Robber 7-Timer
robberTimer.intro: Theoretical 7-frequency: 6/36 = 16.67% (≈1 in 6 rolls). Use the buttons to track your session.

base.name: Catan (Base Game)
base.descriptionTitle: 🏜️ Welcome to the Island of Broken Friendships
base.description: The classic Settlers of Catan — where you'll learn that your closest friends will happily blockade your only road for a single piece of wheat. Collect resources, build roads, settlements and cities, trade with increasingly hostile neighbors, and desperately race to 10 Victory Points before someone drops a Monopoly card on you. Simple rules, infinite grudges.

cards.name: Lumber
cards.description: Produced by Forest hexes when their number is rolled.
usages: Build a Road (Lumber + Brick)
Build a Settlement (Lumber + Brick + Wool + Grain)

cards.name: Brick
cards.description: Produced by Hills hexes when their number is rolled.
usages: Build a Road (Lumber + Brick)
Build a Settlement (Lumber + Brick + Wool + Grain)

cards.name: Wool
cards.description: Produced by Pasture hexes when their number is rolled.
usages: Build a Settlement (Lumber + Brick + Wool + Grain)
Build a City (× in City cost)
Buy a Development Card (Ore + Grain + Wool)

cards.name: Grain
cards.description: Produced by Fields hexes when their number is rolled.
usages: Build a Settlement (Lumber + Brick + Wool + Grain)
Upgrade to City (Ore×3 + Grain×2)
Buy a Development Card (Ore + Grain + Wool)

cards.name: Ore
cards.description: Produced by Mountain hexes when their number is rolled.
usages: Upgrade to City (Ore×3 + Grain×2)
Buy a Development Card (Ore + Grain + Wool)

cards.name: Knight
cards.description: Move the Robber to any terrain hex. Steal one resource card from an adjacent opponent.
usages: Activate before rolling the dice
Collect Largest Army card when you play 3 knights (2 VP)

cards.name: Road Building
cards.description: Place 2 new roads on the board as if you had just built them.
usages: Expand your network quickly
Compete for Longest Road (2 VP when ≥5 roads)

cards.name: Year of Plenty
cards.description: Take any 2 resource cards from the bank. Add them to your hand.
usages: Grab the exact resources you need to build
Complete a combination you're one resource short on

cards.name: Monopoly
cards.description: Name any resource type. All other players must give you all of their cards of that type.
usages: Disrupt an opponent on the verge of a big build
Collect a resource you urgently need

cards.name: Chapel
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. Kept secret until you reveal it to win (or until the game ends).
usages: Count towards 10 VP to win
Play all at once to surprise opponents when claiming victory

cards.name: Great Hall
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. Kept secret until you reveal it to win (or until the game ends).
usages: Count towards 10 VP to win
Play all at once to surprise opponents when claiming victory

cards.name: Library
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. Kept secret until you reveal it to win (or until the game ends).
usages: Count towards 10 VP to win
Play all at once to surprise opponents when claiming victory

cards.name: Market
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. Kept secret until you reveal it to win (or until the game ends).
usages: Count towards 10 VP to win
Play all at once to surprise opponents when claiming victory

cards.name: University
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. Kept secret until you reveal it to win (or until the game ends).
usages: Count towards 10 VP to win
Play all at once to surprise opponents when claiming victory

cards.name: Longest Road
cards.description: Awarded to the first player with a continuous road of 5+ segments. Worth 2 Victory Points. Another player can steal it by building a longer road.
usages: Build roads aggressively to claim the card
Break an opponent's road by placing a settlement in the middle

cards.name: Largest Army
cards.description: Awarded to the first player who plays 3 Knight cards. Worth 2 Victory Points. Another player can steal it by playing more Knights.
usages: Play Knights to control the Robber and earn 2 VP
Protect wheat/ore hexes from the Robber

cards.name: Building Cost Card
cards.description: Reference card showing all construction costs in Catan.
usages: Road: 1 Lumber + 1 Brick
Settlement: 1 Lumber + 1 Brick + 1 Wool + 1 Grain
City: 3 Ore + 2 Grain
Development Card: 1 Ore + 1 Grain + 1 Wool

setup: Assemble the sea frame, place 19 terrain hexes and number discs inside.
Sort resource cards into 5 stacks; shuffle development cards facedown.
Place the Robber on the desert hex.
Each player places 2 settlements and 2 roads (round 1 clockwise, round 2 reverse).
Collect starting resources from hexes adjacent to your second settlement.

turnOverview.title: 1. Production Phase
turnOverview.detail: Optionally play a development card, then roll both dice. All players collect resources from hexes matching the roll. On a 7: players with >7 cards discard half, then move the Robber and steal.

turnOverview.title: 2. Action Phase
turnOverview.detail: Trade with players or the supply (4:1, 3:1, or 2:1 via ports). Build roads, settlements, cities, or buy development cards. Play 1 development card if you didn't before the roll.

keyRules.title: Roads
keyRules.detail: Cost: 1 Lumber + 1 Brick. Must connect to your existing network. Max 15 per player. Worth 0 VP.

keyRules.title: Settlements
keyRules.detail: Cost: 1 Lumber + 1 Brick + 1 Wool + 1 Grain. Must obey distance rule (2 edges from any building). Max 5 per player. Worth 1 VP.

keyRules.title: Cities
keyRules.detail: Cost: 3 Ore + 2 Grain. Upgrade a settlement (produces 2 resources). Max 4 per player. Worth 2 VP.

keyRules.title: Development Cards
keyRules.detail: Cost: 1 Ore + 1 Grain + 1 Wool. Types: Knight (14), Road Building (2), Year of Plenty (2), Monopoly (2), VP cards (5). Cannot play on the turn bought.

keyRules.title: Longest Road
keyRules.detail: First player with 5+ continuous roads gets this tile (2 VP). Can be stolen if another player builds longer.

keyRules.title: Largest Army
keyRules.detail: First player to play 3 Knights gets this tile (2 VP). Can be stolen by playing more.

keyRules.title: Robber (rolling a 7)
keyRules.detail: Players with >7 cards discard half. Active player moves the Robber to a new hex and steals 1 random card from an adjacent player.

keyRules.title: Trading
keyRules.detail: On your turn: trade freely with other players, or trade with the supply at 4:1 (general), 3:1 (any port), or 2:1 (specific port).

scenarios.name: Fixed Setup
scenarios.description: Pre-determined hex and number layout for beginners.
details: Terrain hexes and numbers placed as shown in rulebook
Recommended for first-time players

scenarios.name: Variable Setup
scenarios.description: Randomized board for experienced players.
details: Shuffle sea frame pieces and terrain hexes
Place number discs alphabetically counterclockwise, skip the desert
Players place settlements in two rounds (clockwise then reverse)

buildingCosts.name: Road
buildingCosts.note: Must connect to your network. Max 15 per player.

buildingCosts.name: Settlement
buildingCosts.note: Respect the 2-edge distance rule. Max 5 per player.

buildingCosts.name: City
buildingCosts.note: Upgrades a settlement; produces 2 resources per roll. Max 4 per player.

buildingCosts.name: Development Card
buildingCosts.note: Cannot be played on the turn bought (except VP cards on a winning turn).

trading.title: Bank
trading.detail: Any 4 identical resources for 1 of your choice, anytime on your turn.

trading.title: Generic Harbour
trading.detail: Build a settlement/city on a 3:1 port to trade any 3 identical resources for 1.

trading.title: Specific Harbour
trading.detail: Five resource-specific ports let you swap 2 of that resource for 1 of anything.

trading.title: Player Trade
trading.detail: Negotiate freely with other players during your turn — rates are whatever you can convince them to accept.

reference.tradingNote: You can only trade on your own turn, and only before or after the roll (not during production).

turnFlow.title: Play a dev card (optional)
turnFlow.detail: May be played before rolling if bought on a prior turn.

turnFlow.title: Roll the dice
turnFlow.detail: Sum of both dice determines which hexes produce this turn.

turnFlow.title: Production
turnFlow.detail: Each player collects resources from their settlements (×1) and cities (×2) adjacent to a producing hex.

turnFlow.title: On a 7: Robber
turnFlow.detail: Players with >7 cards discard half (rounded down). Active player moves the robber and steals 1 random card.

turnFlow.title: Trade
turnFlow.detail: Trade with other players, or with the bank/ports at 4:1 / 3:1 / 2:1.

turnFlow.title: Build
turnFlow.detail: Spend resources on roads, settlements, cities, or development cards.

turnFlow.title: Pass
turnFlow.detail: End your turn by passing the dice to the player on your left.

beginner.title: Count the pips, not the numbers
beginner.body: Each number token has small red dots (pips) equal to how many ways it rolls on 2d6. A 6 or 8 is 5 pips; a 2 or 12 is 1 pip. Score a candidate spot by summing its pips — higher is better.

beginner.title: Cover all five resources
beginner.body: With your two starting settlements, aim to touch all five resource types. Missing one forces expensive bank trades (4:1) and slows every build in the midgame.

beginner.title: Wood + brick = early expansion
beginner.body: Roads and settlements both cost wood and brick. A strong first-turn settlement usually sits on decent wood and brick so you can race to a third settlement before the wheat/ore phase begins.

beginner.title: Ports are good, not great
beginner.body: A 2:1 port on a resource you massively over-produce is excellent; a port without the matching production is a VP you could have used on a resource settlement instead.

beginner.title: Dev cards are not free points
beginner.body: Only 5 of ~25 dev cards are VPs. Buy development cards because you want Knights for Largest Army or flexibility — treat the VPs as a bonus, not a plan.

intermediate.title: Commit to Longest Road, or don't
intermediate.body: Longest Road is 2 VP but needs 5+ segments and is vulnerable to a disrupting settlement. Either commit early (wood/brick-heavy start) or skip it entirely — half-investing loses you resources AND the VP.

intermediate.title: Largest Army is cheaper than it looks
intermediate.body: 3 Knights = 2 VP plus ongoing robber control. If your opponents have no knight pressure, you can often claim it uncontested for what amounts to 6 dev-card buys.

intermediate.title: Robber the leader, not the nearest
intermediate.body: The robber is a political weapon. Move it onto the hex producing the most for whoever is closest to winning, not the hex nearest to you. Target the score leader's best production — you'll get repaid in goodwill.

intermediate.title: Anchor high, accept the second offer
intermediate.body: Open trades at rates worse than 1:1. Opponents compare to your anchor, not to the 4:1 bank. You'll often land a 2:1 player trade simply because it looks generous next to your first ask.

intermediate.title: Sometimes the best play is a blocking road
intermediate.body: A road you will never extend can still be worth building if it walls off an opponent's only expansion lane. Cost-benefit: 1 wood + 1 brick vs. denying your rival a 10-pip settlement.

advanced.title: Chase expected value, not variance
advanced.body: Two 4-pip hexes produce the same long-run total as one 8-pip hex, but with less variance — fewer empty turns. For consistent builds, prefer spreading across 3-4 medium numbers over stacking two hot numbers.

advanced.title: Track opponent hands
advanced.body: After each roll, note who collects what. When an opponent has clearly not drawn wheat for 8 rolls, a wheat Monopoly nets you 6-8 cards from the other players instead — retarget on the fly.

advanced.title: Threaten two cities at once
advanced.body: In the endgame, keep 8+ resources that could buy a city upgrade on either of two settlements. Opponents can only robber one — your next turn still builds.

advanced.title: Avoid kingmaker math in 4p
advanced.body: If you can't win this turn, don't hand a free trade to the player about to. Ratio-fair trades with the second-place player are always better than charity to the leader — even if it means you pay 4:1 to the bank.

seafarers.name: Catan: Seafarers
seafarers.descriptionTitle: ⛵ Because One Island Wasn't Enough Drama
seafarers.description: Explore an archipelago of islands because apparently ruining friendships on a single landmass wasn't satisfying enough. Build ships to sail the seas, discover new islands with gold fields (that everyone will immediately fight over), and settle distant shores for bonus VP. Now your road-blocking nemesis can follow you across the ocean.

cards.name: Ship
cards.description: Ships extend your trade network across sea hexes. They work like roads, but only on water edges.
usages: Cost: 1 Lumber + 1 Wool
Placed on a sea edge touching a settlement/city or another ship you own
Counts toward Longest Trade Route
An open-ended ship (not between two of your pieces) can be moved once per turn
A ship cannot be moved on the turn it was built

cards.name: Warship
cards.description: Pirate Islands scenario only. A Knight card converts one of your ships into a warship to fight pirate fortresses.
usages: Pay a Knight card to upgrade 1 ship to a warship
Attack an adjacent pirate fortress: roll dice, a 4–6 damages it
Destroying a fortress scores Victory Points (varies by level)

cards.name: Sea Hex
cards.description: Water hex used in every Seafarers scenario. Ships are placed on sea edges; no resources are produced.
usages: Part of the Seafarers sea frame pieces
Blocks settlements but allows ship edges
The Pirate moves onto sea hexes (never terrain)

cards.name: Gold Field
cards.description: Special terrain that produces a wild resource. When its number is rolled, each adjacent player picks any resource(s) from the bank.
usages: Settlement = 1 free resource of choice
City = 2 free resources of choice
Highly valuable — settle early if possible

cards.name: Fog Hex
cards.description: Fog Islands scenario. Face-down hex revealed the first time a player's ship or road reaches a touching edge.
usages: Reveal a terrain hex → collect 1 matching resource as a reward
Reveal a sea hex → collect 1 gold (any resource of choice)
Adds fog-of-war to exploration

cards.name: Pirate
cards.description: The Pirate replaces (or joins) the Robber on sea hexes. On a 7 or a Knight card you may move EITHER the Robber OR the Pirate.
usages: Move onto a sea hex to steal 1 random resource from a player with an adjacent ship
Blocks opponent ship placement on adjacent edges
Cannot be placed on the hex it is already on

cards.name: Village
cards.description: Forgotten Tribe & Cloth for Catan scenarios. Neutral villages sit on small islands and give cloth tokens when you complete a trade route to them.
usages: First to connect by ship/road earns the cloth stack
Each pair of cloth tokens = 1 Victory Point
Villages run out → can trigger scenario end

cards.name: Cloth Token
cards.description: Round token earned from villages. Collect pairs for VP.
usages: 2 cloth tokens = 1 Victory Point (kept face-down)
Can be stolen by the Pirate in some scenarios

cards.name: Wonder
cards.description: Wonders of Catan scenario. Claim 1 of 5 Wonders (each with prerequisites) and build 4 levels to win.
usages: 5 Wonders, each with entry requirements (e.g. 3 cities, longest road)
Each level costs 5 specific resources
Win by finishing your Wonder OR by hitting 10+ VP while leading on Wonder levels

cards.name: Pirate Fortress
cards.description: Pirate Islands scenario. Each player has a pirate fortress blocking their route home. Destroy yours with warships.
usages: 3 hit points — each successful warship attack removes 1
Destroying yours frees your home island and scores VP
Defender rolls a die: 4–6 destroys the attacking warship

cards.name: New-Island VP
cards.description: Most scenarios award bonus VP for being the FIRST to settle on each new island (or region).
usages: Typically +1 VP or +2 VP per newly-colonised island
Bonus VP tiles are kept face-up next to the board
Multiple players can earn VP from different islands

setup: Lay the special Seafarers sea frame around the play area.
Assemble the scenario map: terrain hexes, sea hexes, ports, gold fields, fog tiles, etc.
Give each player the 15 ships of their colour in addition to standard pieces.
Place the Robber on the desert and the Pirate on its designated sea hex.
Starting placement follows the scenario (may restrict to the main island only).

turnOverview.title: Dice & Production
turnOverview.detail: Roll 2d6. Terrain and Gold Fields produce as normal. Gold fields grant the owner any resource they choose per adjacent building.

turnOverview.title: On a 7
turnOverview.detail: Apply the discard rule (hand > 7 → discard half), then move EITHER the Robber to a terrain hex OR the Pirate to a sea hex. Steal 1 random card from an adjacent player with a building/ship.

turnOverview.title: Trade & Build
turnOverview.detail: Trade with players/bank/ports. Build roads, ships, settlements, cities, dev cards — plus scenario-specific pieces (warships, wonder levels).

turnOverview.title: Move One Ship
turnOverview.detail: Once per turn you may pick up a ship with an open end (not between two of your own pieces) and replant it on any legal sea edge. Cannot move a ship built this turn, and cannot break an already-closed route.

keyRules.title: Ships
keyRules.detail: Cost 1 Lumber + 1 Wool. On sea edges. Combine with roads into one Longest Trade Route (break at your own settlements only in some scenarios).

keyRules.title: Gold Fields
keyRules.detail: When the number is rolled, each adjacent player chooses any 1 resource (2 for a city) from the bank.

keyRules.title: Pirate vs Robber
keyRules.detail: On 7 / Knight, pick ONE. Robber = terrain hex, Pirate = sea hex. Neither may return to the hex it was just on.

keyRules.title: Longest Trade Route
keyRules.detail: Renamed from Longest Road. Mixes roads AND ships. In most scenarios, a chain is broken only by an enemy building, not by switching between road↔ship (which requires your own settlement/city).

keyRules.title: Road Building Card
keyRules.detail: Build any combination of 2 roads and/or ships — 2R, 2S, or 1R + 1S.

keyRules.title: Knight Card
keyRules.detail: Lets you move the Robber OR the Pirate. Still counts toward Largest Army.

keyRules.title: New-Island Bonus
keyRules.detail: First settlement your colour places on an un-colonised island (or region) earns +1 or +2 VP depending on the scenario.

keyRules.title: Shipping Limit
keyRules.detail: Max 15 ships per player. If you run out, you must pick up and reuse an open-ended ship.

scenarios.name: Heading for New Shores
scenarios.description: The flagship introductory scenario. A main island plus several smaller islands across the sea.
details: +2 VP for the FIRST settlement you place on each small island
Two gold-field hexes on the main island
Good scenario to learn ship mechanics

scenarios.name: The Four / Six Islands
scenarios.description: Four (3p) or six (4p) separate islands — no 'main' landmass, every island counts as foreign.
details: +2 VP per foreign island colonised first
Start with 2 ships already on the board
Gold fields scattered for balance

scenarios.name: The Fog Islands
scenarios.description: Unknown terrain hiding under fog tiles. Expand to reveal them.
details: Flip a fog hex when your ship/road touches it
Land reveal → free matching resource; Sea reveal → free gold
+2 VP for first settlement on each fog island

scenarios.name: Through the Desert
scenarios.description: A wide desert strip splits the main island into regions connected by ships.
details: +2 VP per unexplored desert region you settle first
Encourages mixed road + ship networks
Desert itself can be exchanged for a gold field via some variants

scenarios.name: The Forgotten Tribe
scenarios.description: Villages offer cloth and friendship tokens rewarding early exploration.
details: First to connect by ship collects the stack (resources, cloth, dev cards, roads)
Cloth token pairs = VP; friendship tokens are single-use bonuses
Limited stacks — race to reach them

scenarios.name: Cloth for Catan
scenarios.description: Cities on small islands collect cloth when their number is rolled.
details: Every roll adjacent to a villaged small island gives cloth to its owner
2 cloth tokens = 1 VP
Pirate can steal cloth tokens
Game can also end when 5+ village stacks deplete

scenarios.name: The Pirate Islands
scenarios.description: Fight through pirate fortresses to break out of your home island.
details: Each player has their own pirate fortress
Convert ships to warships with Knight cards (1 Knight = 1 upgrade)
Attack with 1 die vs fortress strength; 4–6 damages it
No Longest Trade Route or Largest Army tiles in this scenario

scenarios.name: The Wonders of Catan
scenarios.description: Claim one of five Wonders and race to build all four levels.
details: Each Wonder has prerequisites (e.g. 3 cities, 2:1 wool port)
Claimed Wonders are exclusive
Win by: completing your Wonder, OR hitting 10 VP and leading on levels
Each level costs a fixed 5-resource bill

scenarios.name: New World
scenarios.description: Players design their own random island layout before playing.
details: Players take turns placing hexes during setup
+1 VP per first settlement on each newly-made island
High variance, rewards flexible strategy

reference.tradingNote: Seafarers keeps base-game trading ratios — no new trade rules, but coastal ports remain exceptionally strong when shipping routes connect them.

buildingCosts.name: Road
buildingCosts.note: Counts toward Longest Trade Route alongside ships.

buildingCosts.name: Ship
buildingCosts.note: Max 15 per player. Open-end ship can move once per turn.

buildingCosts.name: Settlement
buildingCosts.note: First on a new island earns the New-Island bonus VP tile.

buildingCosts.name: City
buildingCosts.note: Produces 2 from each adjacent terrain; 2 resources from gold fields.

buildingCosts.name: Development Card
buildingCosts.note: Knight card may move either the Robber or the Pirate.

turnFlow.title: Play a dev card (optional)
turnFlow.detail: Dev cards bought on a previous turn. Knight lets you move Robber OR Pirate.

turnFlow.title: Roll the dice
turnFlow.detail: Terrain produces; gold fields give any resource of choice.

turnFlow.title: On a 7
turnFlow.detail: Discard half if >7. Move Robber OR Pirate and steal from an adjacent player.

turnFlow.title: Trade
turnFlow.detail: Bank 4:1, generic port 3:1, specific port 2:1, player trades anything.

turnFlow.title: Build
turnFlow.detail: Roads, ships, settlements, cities, dev cards — plus scenario pieces.

turnFlow.title: Move one ship (optional)
turnFlow.detail: Pick up an open-end ship (not built this turn) and re-plant anywhere legal.

turnFlow.title: Pass
turnFlow.detail: Pass the dice to the player on your left.

beginner.title: Ships are roads you can pick up
beginner.body: Once per turn, an open-ended ship can be picked up and replanted anywhere legal. Build loose initial ship chains — you can redirect them later toward whichever island your opponents ignore.

beginner.title: Wool + Lumber = expansion fuel
beginner.body: Every ship costs wool + lumber. Starting settlements should touch a reliable wool AND lumber number — otherwise the other players will reach distant islands before you can launch.

beginner.title: Gold fields are premium
beginner.body: A gold-field hex gives any resource you want — equivalent to a 2:1 port on all five resources. A city on a 5/6/8/9 gold hex often wins the game on its own.

beginner.title: Use the Pirate to protect your ore
beginner.body: On a 7, moving the Pirate instead of the Robber is often better defensively: it blocks an opponent ship chain without giving them a sympathy card.

beginner.title: Read the scenario card first
beginner.body: Each Seafarers scenario tweaks VP, setup and win conditions. A 'good' opening in Heading for New Shores is wrong in Wonders of Catan — skim the scenario sheet before placing.

intermediate.title: New-island bonus > 5-point expansion
intermediate.body: A +2 VP island bonus plus a settlement = 3 VP for 4 resources + a few ships. Compared to chasing Longest Route or Largest Army, this is the cheapest VP in the game — just ship earlier than opponents expect.

intermediate.title: Break enemy routes at harbours
intermediate.body: In scenarios that require a settlement to switch between road and ship, drop a settlement on the coastal joint your opponent is building through — it forces them to rebuild their route entirely.

intermediate.title: Pirate Islands: warship before 3rd city
intermediate.body: In Pirate Islands, delaying the warship upgrade loses the race. Swap a city-build turn for 2 warship conversions instead; the fortress VP plus freed shipping lane pay off immediately.

intermediate.title: Reveal fog after you have a port
intermediate.body: In Fog Islands, the first 2–3 reveals are noise. Get a 2:1 port or a gold tile first; each fog reward then compounds because you can convert surplus through the port.

advanced.title: Wonders: commit turn 4 or never
advanced.body: Claiming a Wonder late means you pay 20 resources without time to build — plus your opponents know exactly where to rob. Either claim in the first 4 turns with the prerequisite ready, or play straight-VP and let the Wonder-chasers bleed.

advanced.title: Cloth cities invite the Pirate
advanced.body: Cloth-scoring cities are 2 VP magnets. Keep a ship adjacent to your cloth coast at all times — the pirate steals cloth AND resources there, and a defending ship often deters opponents from repositioning.

advanced.title: Reach the 15-ship cap deliberately
advanced.body: Once capped, every ship you pick up must be repurposed. Plan which segments to leave as 'spare ships' — often around an unused gold field — so you can redeploy 2-3 edges away at a moment's notice.

cities-knights.name: Catan: Cities & Knights
cities-knights.description: A deeper strategic experience for people who thought base Catan was too relaxing. Commodities replace some resources (more things to fight over!), city improvements unlock powerful abilities (that your opponents will use against you), and the Barbarians threaten your cities every single turn — because you needed one more thing to worry about. Features 3 decks of progress cards, knights that constantly need feeding, and the joy of watching your undefended city get pillaged. 13 VP to win, infinite regret along the way and the gold standard of “it’s just a game.”

cards.name: Cloth
cards.description: Commodity produced by upgraded Pasture cities (Metropolis). Used to advance in the Trade column of city improvements.
usages: Produced by cities on Pasture hexes (doubled roll)
Invest in Trade city improvements

cards.name: Coin
cards.description: Commodity produced by upgraded Mountain cities. Used to advance in the Science column.
usages: Produced by cities on Mountain hexes
Invest in Science city improvements

cards.name: Paper
cards.description: Commodity produced by upgraded Forest cities. Used to advance in the Politics column.
usages: Produced by cities on Forest hexes
Invest in Politics city improvements

cards.name: Commercial Harbor
cards.description: Each opponent must choose a commodity card from their hand and give it to you in exchange for a resource card of your choice from your hand.
usages: Force advantageous trades with all opponents
Deplete opponents' commodity reserves

cards.name: Master Merchant
cards.description: Look at the hand cards of an opponent who has a settlement or city adjacent to one of your own. Take 2 resource or commodity cards of your choice from that hand.
usages: Gain intelligence on an opponent's hand
Steal 2 specific cards from a neighbor

cards.name: Merchant
cards.description: Place the Merchant on any land hex adjacent to one of your settlements or cities. While you hold the Merchant, you trade that hex's resource at 2:1. Worth 1 VP.
usages: Gain a 2:1 trade ratio on a chosen resource
Worth 1 VP while you control the Merchant

cards.name: Merchant Fleet
cards.description: Select 1 type of resource or commodity. For the remainder of this turn, you may trade 2 of that type for any 1 other resource or commodity.
usages: Get a temporary 2:1 trade ratio on any type
Great when you have a surplus of one resource

cards.name: Resource Monopoly
cards.description: Name 1 resource type. Each opponent who has 2 or more of that resource must give you exactly 2 of them.
usages: Collect up to 6 resources from 3 opponents
Weaker than base game Monopoly but still powerful

cards.name: Trade Monopoly
cards.description: Name 1 commodity type. Each opponent who has 1 or more of that commodity must give you exactly 1 of them.
usages: Collect commodities from all opponents at once
Disrupts opponents' city improvement plans

cards.name: Alchemist
cards.description: Before rolling the dice on your turn, choose the result of the production dice (both numbers). The event die is still rolled normally.
usages: Guarantee production from your best hexes
Trigger a 7 to activate the robber strategically

cards.name: Crane
cards.description: This turn, you may build one city improvement for 1 fewer commodity than the normal cost.
usages: Save a commodity when upgrading a city track
Reach higher improvement levels faster

cards.name: Engineer
cards.description: Build 1 city wall for free (no resources required). Place it under one of your cities.
usages: Protect a city without spending resources
City walls add +2 to your hand limit when a 7 is rolled

cards.name: Inventor
cards.description: Swap 2 number tokens on the board. You may not swap the tokens with the numbers 2, 6, 8, or 12.
usages: Improve production odds on your hexes
Weaken an opponent's hex by moving a good number away

cards.name: Irrigation
cards.description: Take 2 grain cards from the bank for each of your settlements and cities adjacent to a field hex.
usages: Massive grain boost if well-positioned on fields
Great for fueling knight activations

cards.name: Medicine
cards.description: This turn, you may upgrade one settlement to a city at a reduced cost: only 1 Ore + 1 Grain (instead of 3 Ore + 2 Grain).
usages: Build a city at a fraction of the normal cost
Quickly expand your city network

cards.name: Mining
cards.description: Take 2 ore cards from the bank for each of your settlements and cities adjacent to a mountain hex.
usages: Huge ore gain if you border multiple mountains
Fuel city upgrades and knight recruitment

cards.name: Printer
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. This card remains face-up in front of you for the rest of the game.
usages: Permanent 1 VP — play it immediately
Cannot be stolen or lost

cards.name: Road Building
cards.description: Place 2 roads on the board for free, as if you had just built them.
usages: Expand your network quickly
Compete for Longest Road (2 VP)

cards.name: Smith
cards.description: Promote up to 2 of your knights for free (e.g., Basic → Strong, or Strong → Mighty).
usages: Strengthen your defense without spending resources
Prepare for barbarian attacks efficiently

cards.name: Bishop
cards.description: Move the robber to any hex. Each opponent with a building adjacent to the robber's new hex must give you 1 resource of their choice.
usages: Collect 1 resource from each affected opponent
Relocate the robber to disrupt multiple players

cards.name: Constitution
cards.description: Worth 1 Victory Point. This card remains face-up in front of you for the rest of the game.
usages: Permanent 1 VP — play it immediately
Cannot be stolen or lost

cards.name: Deserter
cards.description: Remove one of an opponent's knights from the board. The opponent takes the knight piece back to their supply.
usages: Weaken an opponent's defense before a barbarian attack
Remove a knight blocking your road

cards.name: Diplomat
cards.description: Remove any open road (a road with at least one end not connected to another road, settlement, or city). If it is your own road, you may immediately place it elsewhere for free.
usages: Relocate one of your own roads strategically
Remove an opponent's road to break their Longest Road

cards.name: Intrigue
cards.description: Displace an opponent's knight from an intersection adjacent to one of your own buildings. The opponent must move their knight to another legal intersection or remove it from the board.
usages: Clear a key intersection for your own use
Force an opponent's knight out of a strategic position

cards.name: Saboteur
cards.description: Each opponent with equal or more victory points than you must discard half of their hand cards (rounded down).
usages: Punish the leading players
Effective when you are behind in VP

cards.name: Spy
cards.description: Look at an opponent's hand of progress cards and steal 1 of your choice.
usages: Gain intelligence and steal a powerful card
Disrupt an opponent's planned strategy

cards.name: Warlord
cards.description: Activate all of your knights for free (without paying grain and ore).
usages: Instantly prepare all knights for barbarian defense
Save significant resources on activations

cards.name: Wedding
cards.description: Each opponent with more victory points than you must give you 2 resource or commodity cards of their choice.
usages: Gain cards from players who are ahead
Most effective when you are trailing in VP

cards.name: Trade (Cloth)
cards.description: You may trade commodities at a 2:1 ratio for any resource or other commodity.
usages: Requires level 3 on the Trade track
Commodity used: Cloth (from Pasture cities)
Level 4 earns the Trade Metropolis (2 VP)

cards.name: Politics (Coin)
cards.description: You may promote strong knights (level 2) to mighty knights (level 3).
usages: Requires level 3 on the Politics track
Commodity used: Coin (from Mountain cities)
Level 4 earns the Politics Metropolis (2 VP)

cards.name: Science (Paper)
cards.description: If you receive no cards during the Production phase, you may take 1 resource card of your choice (except when a 7 is rolled).
usages: Requires level 3 on the Science track
Commodity used: Paper (from Forest cities)
Level 4 earns the Science Metropolis (2 VP)

cards.name: Barbarian Attack
cards.description: Each turn a ship is rolled, the Barbarian token advances. When it reaches Catan, a battle is resolved. If barbarians win, the player with fewest knights loses a city.
usages: Build and activate Knights to defend
City Walls protect one city from being downgraded

setup: Uses the C&K sea frame with barbarian track.
No base game development cards — replaced by progress cards (3 decks).
No Largest Army tile.
Each player's second starting piece is a city (not a settlement).
Each player gets a city improvement board with 3 tracks and 3 cubes.
🥷 Robber starts off the board until the first barbarian attack.

turnOverview.title: 1. Roll Dice + Event Dice
turnOverview.detail: Production dice determines resources. Event dice advances barbarians or triggers progress card draws.

turnOverview.title: 2. Commodities
turnOverview.detail: Cities on mountains produce Coin (+Ore), on pastures produce Cloth (+Wool), on forests produce Paper (+Wood).

turnOverview.title: 3. Action Phase
turnOverview.detail: Trade, build, improve cities, recruit/activate knights, or play progress cards.

keyRules.title: Commodities
keyRules.detail: Cities produce commodities alongside resources: Coin (mountains), Cloth (pastures), Paper (forests). Used to advance city improvement tracks.

keyRules.title: City Improvements
keyRules.detail: 3 tracks — Trade, Science, Politics. Advance by spending commodities. Level 3 unlocks a permanent ability. Level 4 earns a Metropolis (2 VP).

keyRules.title: Metropolis
keyRules.detail: First to level 4 on a track gains that Metropolis (placed on a city, total 4 VP). First to level 5 gets permanent control. Cannot be pillaged.

keyRules.title: Knights
keyRules.detail: 3 strength levels: Basic, Strong, Mighty. Must be recruited then activated (costs 1 Grain + 1 Ore). Can move, displace weaker enemy knights, or chase the robber. All deactivate after barbarian attack.

keyRules.title: Barbarian Attack
keyRules.detail: Barbarian strength = total cities on board. Defender strength = sum of active knights. If barbarians win: player with fewest active knights loses a city (downgraded to settlement). Metropolises are immune.

keyRules.title: City Walls
keyRules.detail: Placed under cities. Each wall adds +2 to your hand limit when a 7 is rolled. Max 3 walls.

keyRules.title: Progress Cards
keyRules.detail: Drawn based on event die + your track level. Hand limit of 4 progress cards. Many unique effects across 3 decks (Trade, Science, Politics).

keyRules.title: The Merchant
keyRules.detail: Placed via progress card on a hex adjacent to your building. Gives 2:1 trade on that resource and is worth 1 VP.

scenarios.name: Standard Game
scenarios.description: The baseline Cities & Knights experience on the standard island.
details: Every city produces commodities alongside resources
Barbarians advance each event-dice barbarian face
3 progress-card decks active: Trade, Science, Politics

scenarios.name: New Shores with C&K
scenarios.description: Combine with Seafarers' Heading for New Shores for city-building across islands.
details: New-island bonus stacks with commodity economy
Gold fields are extremely strong once cities produce coins
Barbarians attack total cities across ALL islands

scenarios.name: 5–6 Player Extension
scenarios.description: Adds two extra players and extra building pieces plus a two-phase Special Build phase.
details: Special Build phase between turns: build only, no dice roll
Progress-card hand limit still 4
Barbarian cadence typically feels faster at 5–6 players

reference.tradingNote: C&K adds commodity trades via the Trade Progress deck and the Merchant token (2:1 on an adjacent hex). Commodities (coin / paper / cloth) also trade 4:1 at the bank.

turnFlow.title: Play a progress card (optional)
turnFlow.detail: Up to 1 progress card before rolling if you have them.

turnFlow.title: Roll production + event die
turnFlow.detail: Red + yellow production dice AND the colored event die (barbarian, town gate, or castle).

turnFlow.title: Production & commodities
turnFlow.detail: Settlements → 1 resource. Cities → 1 resource + 1 commodity from pasture/forest/mountain hexes.

turnFlow.title: Barbarian advance
turnFlow.detail: On a barbarian event die face, the barbarian ship advances. If it reaches Catan, undefended cities become settlements; strongest-knight player wins a Defender of Catan VP.

turnFlow.title: On a 7: Robber
turnFlow.detail: As base game. Knights on the board are unaffected.

turnFlow.title: Trade / Build / Activate knights
turnFlow.detail: Spend resources & commodities. Knights must be activated (1 Grain) before they can move/chase robber.

turnFlow.title: Pass
turnFlow.detail: End turn, hand dice to left.

beginner.title: Race to a second city fast
beginner.body: Only cities produce commodities, and almost every progress-card effect assumes you have commodities to spend. Two cities by turn 5–6 is the C&K version of 'good opening'.

beginner.title: Always have one active knight
beginner.body: An active knight defends Catan during barbarian attacks. Being the sole defender earns Defender of Catan (VP), and being undefended loses you a city — the swing is huge.

beginner.title: Rush your first city before turn 5
beginner.body: In C&K, commodities only flow AFTER you have a city on the matching terrain. Delaying your first city by 2 turns often costs you an entire progress-card deck.

intermediate.title: Pick one track and commit
intermediate.body: Trade, Science and Politics each unlock powerful tempo spikes at levels 3+. Spreading across all three keeps you at level 1 in all — a wasted tempo.

intermediate.title: Don't recruit more knights than you can feed
intermediate.body: Each activation costs 1 Grain + 1 Ore. Three active knights cost you your entire wheat/ore economy — pick 2 and keep them permanent, not 4 hand-to-mouth.

intermediate.title: Science 3 is the cheapest mid-game powerspike
intermediate.body: Medicine (level 3 Science) lets you build cities for 2 Ore + 1 Grain. That's one of the biggest per-resource VP discounts in Catan — prioritise Science until level 3, then branch.

advanced.title: Time the metropolis before barbarians
advanced.body: Reaching track level 4 lets you build a metropolis (2 extra VP). Do it when barbarians are 1–2 rolls from landing: the metropolis city can't be pillaged, so your VP is locked in even if Catan falls.

advanced.title: Sprint to Metropolis 5 before a barbarian loss
advanced.body: A level-4 Metropolis can be stolen if an opponent reaches 5 first. If you lose a city to a barbarian attack in a Metropolis track, you can fall behind irreversibly — hold a spare +1 improvement in hand for defence.

advanced.title: Count barbarians like a 7-robber clock
advanced.body: Each barbarian face advances the ship; 7 advances = attack. Before every event roll, check the tie — if you're the city leader with the fewest knights, you MUST activate one even if it breaks your build.

traders-barbarians.name: Catan: Traders & Barbarians
traders-barbarians.descriptionTitle: 🐫 Five Ways to Complicate a Perfectly Good Board Game
traders-barbarians.description: A collection of five scenarios and variants that add new dimensions to the classic game — because someone at Catan Studio looked at the base game and thought 'needs more camels.' Features trade routes where you deliver goods across the island like an unpaid intern, barbarian invasions that keep you on your toes, and enough variant rules to make your rulebook thicker than a phone book. Perfect for the group that's played base Catan 200 times and needs fresh reasons to argue.

cards.name: Camel / Caravan
cards.description: Moves goods tokens along your road network to the friendly village or chapel target in the Traders & Barbarians main scenario.
usages: Moves up to 4 road segments per turn (scenario-dependent)
Delivering a goods token scores VP
Blocked by enemy buildings and the robber

cards.name: Gold Coin Token
cards.description: Earned by delivering goods or via Event Cards. Works like a wild resource in several scenarios.
usages: Spend as any 1 resource
In some scenarios, 2 gold tokens can be redeemed for 1 VP
Stored face-up in front of you — other players can see your stockpile

cards.name: Goods Token
cards.description: Wheat, cloth, and coin goods waiting to be hauled by caravans. Earned when a matching city rolls.
usages: Cloth → produced by cities on pastures
Coin → produced by cities on mountains
Wheat goods → produced by cities on fields
Loaded into caravans at the producing city

cards.name: Barbarian Knight
cards.description: Used in the Barbarian Attack scenario. Hire and move knights to defend the island from barbarian landing sites.
usages: Hire cost: 1 Grain + 1 Ore
Move 1 hex per turn
Combat is resolved by comparing knight counts on the barbarian hex

setup: Modular expansion — 4 standalone variants and 5 standalone scenarios.
Variants and scenarios can be mixed and matched.
Includes a 2-player variant with neutral players.

keyRules.title: Modular Design
keyRules.detail: Each variant/scenario adds or changes specific rules. Pick and combine freely with the base game.

keyRules.title: Caravans (Main scenario)
keyRules.detail: Load goods at producing cities, move up to 4 road segments per turn, unload at the friendly village to score.

keyRules.title: Event Cards (variant)
keyRules.detail: Use the 37-card event deck instead of rolling. Each card shows a production number PLUS an event (earthquake, good-harvest, epidemic, etc.).

keyRules.title: Barbarian Attack scenario
keyRules.detail: Barbarian pieces land on coastal hexes. Hire knights (1 Grain + 1 Ore) and move them 1 hex per turn to defend. A successful defense scores VP.

keyRules.title: Rivers variant
keyRules.detail: Rivers replace 7 edge-segments. Crossing requires a bridge (2 Brick + 1 Lumber). First bridge = 1 VP.

keyRules.title: Fishing variant
keyRules.detail: Lake hex instead of the desert. Fish tokens spent to: remove robber (2), steal a card (3), free resource (4), free road (5), free dev card (7). The 'old boot' token is passed to whoever is ahead — holder needs +1 VP to win.

scenarios.name: The Friendly Robber
scenarios.description: The Robber cannot be moved to a hex with a building of a player who only has 2 VP.
details: Protects beginners from early-game targeting

scenarios.name: Harbors of Catan
scenarios.description: Strongest Ports tile (2 VP) for the player with most VP in buildings on ports.
details: Encourages port building strategy

scenarios.name: CATAN Event Cards
scenarios.description: 37-card event deck replaces dice rolls.
details: Cards have production numbers + special events
Events include Earthquake, Epidemic, Good Neighbors
New Year card triggers deck reshuffle

scenarios.name: CATAN for Two
scenarios.description: 2-player variant with neutral players.
details: Double production phase (roll twice)
Trade tokens for forced trades or moving the robber
Must build for neutral players when you build

scenarios.name: Fishing on Catan
scenarios.description: Lake hex replaces desert with fishing grounds on coasts.
details: Fish tokens earned from production
Spend fish: remove robber (2), steal card (3), free resource (4), free road (5), free dev card (7)
Old boot = need +1 VP to win

scenarios.name: Rivers of Catan
scenarios.description: River tiles replace 7 hexes. Bridges cross rivers.
details: Bridge cost: 2 Brick + 1 Wood
Gold coins from river tiles
Wealthiest Catanian (1 VP) / Poorest (-2 VP)

scenarios.name: Merchant Trains
scenarios.description: Wagons form trains from the watering hole.
details: Wagons collected when building, placed via voting
Buildings between 2 wagons = +1 VP each
Wagons count toward Longest Route

scenarios.name: Barbarian Attack
scenarios.description: Castle hex on board. Knights defend against barbarian landings.
details: No robber or base development cards
Knights placed on hex edges
3 barbarians conquer a hex; prisoners = VP

scenarios.name: Trade Routes
scenarios.description: 3 commodity hexes. Wagons transport goods between them.
details: Wagon upgrades increase movement
Gold earned from deliveries
Barbarians block roads

reference.tradingNote: T&B introduces caravans, trade routes and gold — some scenarios replace the standard bank trade with delivery-based VP. Always check the specific scenario rules before assuming 4:1 still applies.

trading.title: Caravans (scenario)
trading.detail: Move caravans up to 3 road segments per turn; deliver goods tokens to the friendly village for a VP.

trading.title: Gold (scenario)
trading.detail: Trade gold tokens earned from rivers or trade routes at 2:1 at the bank.

beginner.title: Plan the caravan route first
beginner.body: In Traders & Barbarians, the village-route geometry decides your opening. Your first two settlements should touch the road path your caravans will travel, not necessarily the best pip hexes.

beginner.title: Start with 1 variant at a time
beginner.body: T&B variants stack, but layering Rivers + Fish + Events at once makes rules-reference drag the game. Play each variant alone first; mix only after everyone knows the ruleset.

intermediate.title: Use barbarians as a knight
intermediate.body: In the Barbarian Attack variant, you can march barbarians toward an opponent's cluster of settlements for a fee. The fee pays for itself if it stalls their biggest producer by even one turn.

intermediate.title: Caravans need a shielded road path
intermediate.body: A single enemy settlement can block your entire caravan route. Run two parallel road branches — one direct, one scenic — so a blocker costs 1 turn rather than the whole delivery.

advanced.title: Bank gold, don't spend it
advanced.body: Gold converts at 2:1. In scenarios with river gold, holding 8+ gold lets you assemble a city or dev card on demand without revealing the resource mix to opponents — pure information advantage.

advanced.title: Event deck flattens dice but sharpens planning
advanced.body: With the event deck, every production number appears almost the same number of times per shuffle. Plan ~12 rolls ahead — if 8 hasn't come up, it's mathematically DUE, not superstitiously due.

explorers-pirates.name: Catan: Explorers & Pirates
explorers-pirates.descriptionTitle: 🏴‍☠️ Catan Goes Full Columbus (Minus the Controversy)
explorers-pirates.description: Set sail to explore unknown waters because sitting on a known island was getting boring. Build ships and harbors, complete missions like spice trade or fish hauls (yes, fish — peak adventure), and colonize new lands far from home. Think of it as Seafarers' ambitious older sibling who studied abroad and won't stop talking about it. More moving pieces, more missions, more 'wait, whose turn is it?' moments.

cards.name: Harbor Settlement
cards.description: A settlement built on a coast marker. Worth 2 VP (not 1). Required to build ships from that intersection.
usages: Worth 2 VP
Only type that can build ships
Can load/unload settlers, crews, spice sacks, fish hauls
Holds 1 large piece or 2 small pieces at a time

cards.name: Ship
cards.description: Ships carry cargo (settlers, crews, spice, fish) around the sea. Unlike Seafarers, E&P ships MOVE — they have movement points.
usages: Cost: 1 Lumber + 1 Wool
Start of turn: every ship has 4 Movement Points
Spend 1 Wool on any ship to give it +2 MP this turn
Up to 2 ships on the same sea edge
Carry up to 2 small pieces (settlers/crews) or 1 spice sack

cards.name: Settler
cards.description: A small wooden piece you load onto a ship, transport, and unload at a coastal intersection to place a settlement.
usages: Recruit cost: 1 Grain + 1 Wool (at your harbour settlement)
Unload a settler at a coastal land intersection → place a settlement (free)
Must satisfy the 2-edge distance rule
If the target hex is not yet discovered, flip it and gain the reveal bonus

cards.name: Crew
cards.description: Small piece used to attack pirate lairs and work spice farms.
usages: Recruit cost: 1 Grain + 1 Ore (at your harbour settlement)
3 crews placed on a pirate lair → capture it (1 VP)
1 crew on a spice farm → activates it to harvest a spice sack

cards.name: Gold Coin
cards.description: Universal currency in E&P. Each player starts with 2 gold; extras are awarded for no-production turns and exploration.
usages: 2 gold → 1 resource of choice (bank trade, max twice per turn)
1 gold tribute to move a ship past the pirate ship
Earn 1 gold if you produced zero resources on a dice roll
Earn 2 gold when you reveal a sea hex

cards.name: Fish Haul
cards.description: Token earned by rolling the right number on your movement die next to a fish shoal. Delivered to the Council of Catan to advance the fish mission.
usages: Catch: move a ship next to a shoal, roll die, match the shoal → take a fish haul
Carry: 1 fish haul takes one small slot on a ship
Deliver to the Council → advance 1 step on the fish mission track

cards.name: Spice Sack
cards.description: Large token harvested from an activated spice farm. Worth more than fish — one sack fills an entire ship.
usages: Place a crew on a farm → pick up a spice sack next turn
Fills a ship (no other cargo)
Deliver to the Council → advance 1 step on the spice mission track

cards.name: Pirate Ship
cards.description: On a 7, the active player places the pirate ship. Unlike Seafarers, the E&P pirate affects movement and trade, not settlements.
usages: Blocks ship movement: pay 1 gold to pass through
Steal 1 random card from each player with an adjacent ship
A stationary ship adjacent to the pirate may attempt to chase it (roll a die, 6 chases away)

cards.name: Pirate Lair
cards.description: Neutral pirate fortresses placed on gold-field hexes. Conquer by unloading crews; each captured lair scores 1 VP.
usages: 3 crews must be dropped on a lair to capture it
Capture = 1 VP + advance on the pirate mission track
Gold field becomes yours to harvest on subsequent rolls

cards.name: Fish Shoal
cards.description: Neutral shoal tokens placed on sea hexes in the fish scenario. A matching movement-die roll next to a shoal yields a fish haul.
usages: Move ship adjacent → roll movement die
Shoal number matches → claim 1 fish haul
Shoals eventually deplete

cards.name: Spice Farm
cards.description: Neutral farms on remote islands in the spice scenario. Activate with a crew and harvest spice sacks on later turns.
usages: Drop 1 crew → farm is activated for you
Next turn: collect a spice sack
Activating a farm grants a permanent bonus (Swift Voyage, Pirate Bonus or Fast Gold)

cards.name: Council of Catan
cards.description: The hex on the starting island where you deliver fish hauls and spice sacks to advance their mission tracks.
usages: Unload a fish haul at the Council → +1 step on fish track
Unload a spice sack at the Council → +1 step on spice track
Mission tracks grant escalating VP bonuses

cards.name: Spice Haul
cards.description: Deliver spice sacks to the Council to climb the spice mission track. Top players earn VP and special abilities.
usages: Each spice sack = 1 step forward
Advancement tiles grant VP and one-time bonuses
Only the leaders on the track score the big VP rewards at game end

cards.name: Fish for Catan
cards.description: Deliver fish hauls to feed the Council. Cheaper and faster than spice, but the rewards scale smaller.
usages: Each fish haul = 1 step forward
First deliveries grant small resource bonuses; later ones grant VP
Good mid-priority mission alongside one 'main' track

cards.name: Pirate Lairs
cards.description: Capture pirate lairs with crews to advance. Combat-oriented track — the fastest to VP if you have the ore.
usages: Each captured lair = 1 step + 1 VP on the spot
Hero (highest-rolling crew) advances extra
Top spot grants permanent Largest-Army-style bonuses

setup: Major departure from base game: no classic development cards, no cities, no robber.
Lay the starting island + 2 unexplored regions (face-down hexes, with number discs placed beside them).
Each player's FIRST piece is a harbour settlement (on a coast marker, 2 VP); SECOND piece is a regular settlement.
Each player receives 2 gold coins and the reference card for the chosen scenario.
No ports — all bank trades go through the 3:1 mystery supply, or 2-gold → 1-resource (max twice per turn).

turnOverview.title: 1. Roll & Produce
turnOverview.detail: Roll 2d6. Adjacent settlements and harbour settlements collect 1 resource. Any player who collected NOTHING takes 1 gold coin instead.

turnOverview.title: 2. On a 7
turnOverview.detail: Discard half if >7 cards. Move the pirate ship; steal 1 random card from every player with an adjacent ship.

turnOverview.title: 3. Trade & Recruit
turnOverview.detail: Trade with players, supply (3:1), or gold coins (2 gold → 1 resource, ×2). Recruit settlers (Grain+Wool) and crews (Grain+Ore) at your harbour settlements.

turnOverview.title: 4. Build
turnOverview.detail: Build ships (Lumber+Wool), settlements (Lumber+Brick+Wool+Grain), or harbour settlements (free upgrade of a coastal settlement via Lumber+Brick+Wool+Grain + 1 gold).

turnOverview.title: 5. Ship Movement
turnOverview.detail: Every ship has 4 MP. Pay 1 Wool per ship for +2 MP. Load/unload at harbour settlements or target intersections. Exploration is free on reveal.

turnOverview.title: 6. Unload & Score
turnOverview.detail: Unload settlers/crews/fish/spice to place settlements, capture lairs, activate farms, or advance missions. End turn.

keyRules.title: Harbour Settlements
keyRules.detail: 2 VP. Only type that builds/repairs ships and holds multiple cargo pieces. Built on coast markers — limited placements per board.

keyRules.title: Ships
keyRules.detail: Cost Lumber + Wool. 4 MP per turn; +2 MP per Wool spent. Max 2 ships per edge. Carry up to 2 small pieces OR 1 spice sack.

keyRules.title: Exploration
keyRules.detail: Moving adjacent to a face-down hex flips it. Terrain hex gets its number disc and you gain 1 matching resource. Sea hex grants 2 gold.

keyRules.title: Settlers
keyRules.detail: 1 Grain + 1 Wool. Carried on ships. Unload at a coastal intersection to place a free settlement (respect distance rule).

keyRules.title: Crews
keyRules.detail: 1 Grain + 1 Ore. Used to capture pirate lairs (3 crews each) or activate spice farms (1 crew each).

keyRules.title: Pirate Ship
keyRules.detail: Moved on a 7. Passing through costs 1 gold. Steals a random card from every adjacent ship owner (not just one).

keyRules.title: Chase the Pirate
keyRules.detail: A stationary ship adjacent to the pirate may roll a die at end of turn; a 6 chases it to a new sea hex.

keyRules.title: Missions
keyRules.detail: Full game uses 3 parallel mission tracks (spice, fish, pirate lairs). Advancement tiles and top-of-track bonuses contribute heavily to the 17 VP goal.

scenarios.name: Land Ho!
scenarios.description: Introductory scenario — learn exploration, shipping and settlers without missions.
details: Discover unknown terrain with ships
Transport settlers and build settlements on new islands
No crews, no missions, no pirate lairs

scenarios.name: Pirate Lairs
scenarios.description: Capture pirate lairs scattered on gold hexes. Introduces crews and the pirate lair mission.
details: 3 crew drops = captured lair (1 VP each)
Hero advance: highest-rolling crew gives an extra step
Top player on the pirate mission track gets a +1 Largest-Army-style tile

scenarios.name: Fish for Catan
scenarios.description: Catch fish and deliver them to the Council — introduces the fish mission.
details: Roll the movement die next to a fish shoal; matching number = haul
Deliver hauls to the Council of Catan to advance the fish track
Shoals deplete — the race is real

scenarios.name: Spices for Catan
scenarios.description: Activate distant spice farms with crews and deliver spice sacks — introduces the spice mission.
details: Activated farms grant one of: Swift Voyage, Pirate Bonus, Fast Gold
Each sack fills a full ship
Top spot on the spice track is worth the most single VP reward in the game

scenarios.name: Explorers & Pirates (Full)
scenarios.description: The complete experience — all three missions running in parallel.
details: All three mission tracks (fish, spice, pirate lairs) active
Mission tiles + settlement VP + harbour settlement VP = 17 VP
Recommended 3–4 players with experience in the intro scenarios

reference.tradingNote: E&P keeps base bank/harbour ratios but adds mission-driven trades (fish for Council VPs, spice sacks for mission progress). Treat mission rewards as better than any bank trade when feasible.

buildingCosts.name: Ship
buildingCosts.note: 4 MP per turn. +2 MP per Wool. Max 2 per edge.

buildingCosts.name: Settlement
buildingCosts.note: Typically placed by unloading a settler from a ship.

buildingCosts.name: Harbor Settlement
buildingCosts.note: Upgrade a coastal settlement. Needed to build ships. +1 VP vs settlement.

buildingCosts.name: Settler
buildingCosts.note: Recruited at a harbour settlement. Unloaded to place a settlement.

buildingCosts.name: Crew
buildingCosts.note: Captures pirate lairs and activates spice farms.

trading.title: Supply
trading.detail: Any 3 identical resources → 1 of your choice, anytime on your turn. No classic ports exist in E&P.

trading.title: Gold Coin
trading.detail: Spend 2 gold for any 1 resource. Max twice per turn.

trading.title: Council of Catan
trading.detail: Deliver fish hauls or spice sacks to the Council to advance mission tracks. Mission tiles are worth more than any resource trade.

turnFlow.title: 1. Roll & Production
turnFlow.detail: Roll 2d6. Settlements (×1) and harbour settlements (×1) collect adjacent terrain. No production → 1 gold.

turnFlow.title: 2. On a 7
turnFlow.detail: Discard half if >7. Move the pirate ship. Steal 1 card from each adjacent ship owner.

turnFlow.title: 3. Trade & Recruit
turnFlow.detail: Supply trade 3:1. Gold trade 2:1 (×2). Recruit settlers and crews at harbour settlements.

turnFlow.title: 4. Build
turnFlow.detail: Ships · settlements · harbour settlements. No cities, no classic dev cards.

turnFlow.title: 5. Move Ships
turnFlow.detail: Each ship has 4 MP. Pay 1 Wool for +2 MP. Carry cargo, explore, fight, deliver.

turnFlow.title: 6. Unload & Score
turnFlow.detail: Drop settlers → settlements. Drop crews → capture lairs / activate farms. Deliver fish & spice to the Council.

beginner.title: Pick two missions — skip the third
beginner.body: Spreading across fish, spice and pirate lairs produces thin progress everywhere. Commit to two; the third usually rewards whoever's behind on the others.

beginner.title: Plan your harbour settlement positions early
beginner.body: Harbour settlements are 2 VP AND the only piece that can build ships or recruit. Scout the coast markers before placing — a buried harbour kills your expansion tempo.

beginner.title: 4 MP rarely reaches the goal — plan the +2 MP upfront
beginner.body: Budget 1 Wool per ship per long trip. A ship without a boost often strands on the sea, wasting a round of movement and a piece of cargo.

beginner.title: Exploration is basically free VP
beginner.body: Each reveal gives you a resource or 2 gold before the hex even starts producing. Point your early ships at fog tiles — you'll fund your next settler from the rewards alone.

intermediate.title: Crew > speed until the endgame
intermediate.body: Double crew + 4 MP delivers two cargo per trip. Only trade crews for extra MP in the last 4 turns when you absolutely must reach a distant spice farm in time.

intermediate.title: Park a defensive ship next to the pirate
intermediate.body: A stationary ship next to the pirate has a 1-in-6 shot to chase it each turn — and passing costs opponents 1 gold. Over a 3-turn window you either clear it or tax your opponents 3 coins.

intermediate.title: Use both gold trades every turn you need to
intermediate.body: 2 gold → 1 resource twice per turn is your port substitute. Hoarding gold doesn't help — spending it to unlock a build 2 turns earlier usually wins more VP than the saved coin.

advanced.title: Block first, capture later
advanced.body: 3 crews for a full lair capture is expensive. Drop a single crew to park on it first: that alone delays your opponent's capture by a turn, often long enough for you to reach it with the final crews.

advanced.title: Split ships: one carrier, one explorer
advanced.body: Use one low-crew ship purely as a discoverer (it reveals hexes cheaply) and a second fully-loaded ship as your delivery barge. Don't ask one ship to do both; you lose a full cargo slot per trip.

advanced.title: Plan the 17-VP total before turn 5
advanced.body: Two harbour settlements (4) + 5 settlements (5) + 2 mission top tiles (6) + 2 lairs (2) = 17. Know the exact path and don't chase pieces that don't slot into it.
